but then you kind of assign 20K a month to it, right? So that's what I mean by numbers assigned to what they want. And we also, this is not always essential, but this is what I call widening the gap. So the money belief is the belief that the prospect has the resources, one, and willingness, two, to solve the problem, okay? So there's two parts to this. If they don't have the resources, that's a condition, not an objection. That means like they physically do not have the money. They have a 450 credit score, $750 in the bank and only 500 coming in next month. So the way we know that it's a condition, not an objection, that it's a resources thing is through the open wallet trick, which means we take them through the entire sales process, we drop the investment at the end, we tie it down on the certainty that this is the right thing, now is the right time, we get them super certain that I wanna do this is just the money, we isolate the money objection and then we use a framework, you're gonna learn later in the training to get their exact cash on hand, their checking account, their savings account, what exactly is in there, their credit score, the amount of money that's coming in in the next 30 days. ## [24:08 - 27:09]

when we get into the sales process and the discovery phase of the sales process and also how to take notes on your sales calls. Okay, so we're gonna do that through what's called Socratic Dialog. Now, I don't know if this is the exact definition of Socratic Dialog, but for our purposes, we're gonna define Socratic Dialog as the process of asking skilled questions to help the prospect come to the conclusion that we want them to, but as if it's their own idea. So what we're really doing here is leading them to the logical and emotional conclusion that we want, but with them feeling like they're totally in control and like it's their own idea, okay? And what that's gonna do is it's gonna create a lot of internal pressure, because they're gonna feel like they're doing things for their own reasons opposed to external pressure where they're feeling cornered or like we're pressuring them into doing something, okay? Now, the reason why Socratic Dialog is so powerful is something called consistency bias. So when we're asking our skilled questions and getting the prospect to express the above seven beliefs, not only are they telling us those things, but more importantly, they're telling themselves, okay? And the reason why that's so important is the highest driver of our human behavior is to appear consistent. Now read influenced by Robert Chaudhouni for more on this, but in his book, the first chapter, the highest driver of human behavior is what's called the consistency bias and the drive to appear consistent. So when we use Socratic Dialog in conjunction with the belief ladder and everything I'm gonna teach you, it's simply gonna feel incongruent for the prospect not to move forward at the end of the call because of how much consistency bias we've created. And that's how we create the objectionless closed where instead of having to hard close the prospect, the prospect's closing themselves. Okay, and it's important to understand if you look at most great closers, while they certainly can handle objections, most of their closes are objectionless or maybe they're just handling some logistics or negotiating a payment plan, you know, negotiating timing, something like that. But most of them like, they're not trying to build certainty at the close or handle a bunch of objections or having these crazy come to Jesus's.
